cataloging_subject.label: Piers Plowman (B version)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged intellectual unit is the B version of the Middle English alliterative dream vision commonly called Piers Plowman. It includes its Prologue and twenty Passus, but excludes the distinct A and C versions, the broader Piers Plowman tradition, Trinity College Cambridge MS B.15.17 as a physical codex, A. V. C. Schmidt's 1978 critical edition and apparatus, the U-M/OTA electronic encoding, and the repository XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: Piers Plowman (B version)
catalog_summary.author: William Langland (attributed)
catalog_summary.region: Associated linguistically with the South West Midlands; exact place of composition unknown

titles.supplied_by: cataloger from current scholarship and source-edition identification

assertion.notes: The parenthetical version label fixes the cataloging boundary and is not presented as a medieval title.

titles.supplied_by: A. V. C. Schmidt's 1978 source edition and the U-M/OTA header

agents.name: William Langland
notes: The conventional author name is retained with attributed status. Scholarship generally treats A, B, and C as versions by one author named or pen-named William Langland, but the biographical identity remains uncertain.

agents.name: A. V. C. Schmidt
notes: Editor of the exact 1978 critical B-text used by the U-M/OTA encoding; not the medieval author.

assertion.notes: This records the dominant scholarly attribution without converting an uncertain medieval author identity into a biographically established fact.

scope.description: Editorial responsibility for the 1978 source edition represented by the encoding, not responsibility for the medieval work
Selected variant readings in the 1978 critical edition; the contributing witness sigla and supplied ranges remain unverified from a complete copy

assertion.notes: This is the composition range conventionally assigned to the B version, not the date of Trinity B.15.17, Schmidt's edition, or the electronic encoding.

places.label: South West Midlands
notes: A broad linguistic association in scholarship, not a claim that the B version was composed at one identified modern locality.

assertion.notes: The evidence concerns the poem's linguistic and authorial associations; it does not establish the place of composition or the copying place of Trinity B.15.17.

language_statements.label: Latin

scope.description: Intermittent quotations, tags, and continuations embedded within the Middle English verse

notes: The XML contains hundreds of line elements with marked Latin content; many share a lineation number with the adjacent Middle English verse and must not be counted mechanically as additional canonical lines.
A brief French or Anglo-Norman inset also occurs, but it is retained as a note rather than promoted to a whole-work discovery language from one isolated quotation.

form_statements.description: A long alliterative poem organized as a Prologue and twenty Passus, with embedded Latin quotations and occasional non-verse textual artifacts in the electronic line-tag stream.

subject_statements.label: Social estates, law, exploitation, and institutional corruption

summaries.value: In a sequence of dreams, the wanderer Will searches for the meaning of Christian truth and for a way to live justly. Allegorical figures and social estates debate truth, labor, charity, confession, law, learning, poverty, clerical authority, and salvation; the poem's successive quests for Dowel, Dobet, and Dobest culminate in a vision of Christ, the founding and corruption of the Barn of Unity, and Conscience setting out again to seek Piers the Plowman.

The represented B version consists of a Prologue followed by Passus I–XX. The source XML maps this poem to Schmidt's printed pp. 1–263, while Trinity College Cambridge MS B.15.17 preserves its base-witness text at ff. 1r–130v; f. 131r begins a different codex item.

The source header calls the item The Vision of Piers Plowman and identifies Schmidt's 1978 critical B-text based on Trinity B.15.17 with selected variant readings. This record therefore catalogs the B-version work, while the manuscript, edition, encoding, and repository artifact remain separate lineage entities; it does not make claims about all A-, B-, and C-version readings.

extent.description: The extent models one verse work divided structurally into one Prologue and twenty Passus. The encoding has 7,598 nonempty L elements and 7,256 distinct section-plus-N lineation keys. There are 342 additional L elements sharing a key, chiefly marked Latin continuations; a running-title artifact, The Vision of Piers Plowman, is also encoded as an L at printed p. 188. Summing the terminal N values of the 21 sections gives 7,275 numbered positions, but 19 positions are absent. Because raw tags, distinct N keys, and numbered spans are not equivalent intellectual line counts, and the complete 1978 edition was not publicly inspectable, no canonical verse-line total is asserted.

content_parts.label: B-version poem: Prologue and Passus I–XX
content_parts.summary: The complete B-version work represented in the encoding, from the Prologue's field full of folk through Conscience's final departure to seek Piers the Plowman.

locators.value: canvases 5–135 of 156; canvas 135 is the shared f. 130v–f. 131r spread

notes: The twenty-one-section summary follows the represented edition and XML hierarchy; it does not attempt to model every dream subdivision or every editorial line-number anomaly.

related_works.title: Piers Plowman
notes: This abstract related-work entry represents the broader A/B/C-version poem and tradition; it is not a claim that one recoverable archetypal text contains all three versions.

evidence.summary: Identifies PPlLan and OTA U-1687-A, names Langland and Schmidt's 1978 critical B-text based on Trinity B.15.17 with selected variants, dates the electronic file to 1993 and the source creation to 1377–1379, states the omitted front/back matter and editorial notes, and preserves the Prologue plus twenty Passus.
notes: Git blob 293d11257ad431f4c652fb8ae6cbeb22d22957cd at CME submodule commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.

evidence.citation: Direct XML structure and lineation audit of PPlLan.xml.
evidence.locator: All DIV1, L, HI1, PB, P, and N attributes
evidence.summary: Counts one Prologue, twenty Passus, 7,598 nonempty L elements, 7,256 distinct section-plus-N keys, 342 duplicate-key L elements across 288 keys, 397 L elements with marked HI1 content, 261 PB milestones spanning printed pp. 1–263, and a running-title L artifact at printed p. 188. Terminal N spans total 7,275 positions with 19 absent positions.
notes: The audit distinguishes XML element count, distinct lineation keys, numbered spans, and intellectual verse-line extent.

evidence.citation: Piers Plowman Electronic Archive, About Piers Plowman.
evidence.summary: Describes the fourteenth-century alliterative allegorical dream vision attributed to William Langland, the A, B, and C lifetime versions, the B version's broad circulation, and the poem's religious, social, and institutional concerns.

evidence.citation: Piers Plowman Electronic Archive, Trinity College Cambridge MS B.15.17 (W), Introduction.
evidence.summary: Identifies W as Trinity College Cambridge MS B.15.17, describes Booklet 1 at ff. 1–130 as a B-version witness and Booklet 2 at ff. 131–147 as other texts, and records W's central role as a base manuscript for the Wright, Kane-Donaldson, and Schmidt editions.

evidence.citation: Piers Plowman Electronic Archive, Bodleian Library MS Laud Misc. 581 (F), Introduction.
evidence.summary: Provides a cautious account of Langland attribution, the poem's South West Midlands linguistic association and London connections, and the complex B-version manuscript tradition.
notes: Used for work-level linguistic context, not as evidence that the F witness was a source of Schmidt's W-based text.

evidence.citation: Lynn Staley Johnson, Langland, Piers Plowman, in Images of Kingship in Chaucer and His Ricardian Contemporaries, Cambridge University Press.
evidence.summary: Dates the B text to 1377–1379 and distinguishes it from the poem's other versions.

evidence.citation: The Piers Plowman tradition, in The Cambridge History of Medieval English Literature, Cambridge University Press.
evidence.summary: Summarizes the prevailing view that the versions are by a single author named or pen-named William Langland while preserving the uncertainty of the biographical attribution.

evidence.citation: WorldCat, The Vision of Piers Plowman, OCLC 5221733.
evidence.summary: Confirms A. V. C. Schmidt as editor, the 1978 J. M. Dent and E. P. Dutton London/New York publication, the critical B-text based on Trinity College Cambridge MS B.15.17 with selected variant readings, and ISBN 046010571X.
notes: Public catalogs disagree slightly over the preliminary pagination; no pagination claim beyond the encoded work-text range is based on that discrepancy.

evidence.citation: Trinity College Cambridge, Medieval Manuscripts Catalogue, MS B.15.17 (James 353).
evidence.summary: Describes the fourteenth-century parchment codex, 147 folios, dimensions, language, collation, and contents: Piers Plowman at ff. 1–130v, followed by a Treatise of Sin and a short poem at ff. 131–147.

evidence.citation: Trinity College Cambridge, MS B.15.17, IIIF Presentation manifest.
evidence.summary: Provides the 156-canvas complete color facsimile and item-level attribution and CC BY-NC 4.0 image license; canvas labels map the Piers Plowman portion to canvases 5–135.

evidence.citation: Direct inventory and visual inspection of the cached Trinity College Cambridge MS B.15.17 IIIF bundle.
evidence.summary: Verified all 156 inventory image checksums and visually inspected the covers and work boundaries. Canvas 5 is the opening f. 1r text leaf; canvas 135 is the shared f. 130v–f. 131r spread, with the end of Piers Plowman on the left and the next codex item beginning on the right.

open_questions.question: How should the XML's 7,598 L elements, 7,256 distinct section-plus-N keys, 7,275 numbered-span positions, and omitted or duplicate labels be reconciled with Schmidt's intended lineation and a defensible canonical B-version line count?
next_steps: Inspect a complete copy of Schmidt's exact 1978 edition, including its textual notes and lineation explanation.
Compare a sample of duplicate-key Latin continuations, missing N positions, and the p. 188 running-title artifact against the printed pages.
Keep verse_lines null until an intellectual count can be distinguished from XML tag and numbering counts.

open_questions.question: Which additional witnesses supply Schmidt's selected variant readings, and at which passages do those readings enter the 1978 text or apparatus?
next_steps: Consult Schmidt's complete introduction, list of manuscripts, and apparatus in the exact 1978 edition.
Do not model unnamed witnesses as direct source entities or infer passage ranges from the subtitle alone.

notes: The preferred title and cataloging subject make the B-version boundary explicit; A and C are related versions, not unmodeled portions of this item.
Langland is retained as an attributed author. The wording does not claim that the conventional name is a securely documented biography.
Trinity B.15.17 is the principal base witness of Schmidt's critical text, but Schmidt's selected variants make the edition non-diplomatic.
The twenty-one-section and XML line-tag summaries are navigational descriptions, not claims that electronic element boundaries are medieval authorial or canonical line counts.
The complete cached codex contains the work at ff. 1r–130v and also preserves covers and the subsequent texts through f. 147; those adjacent materials are outside the cataloged work.
